attributes.stem:
A 62-year-old man presents to the day procedure unit for a scheduled colonoscopy after a positive faecal occult blood test as part of the national bowel cancer screening programme. He has been otherwise asymptomatic. His medical history includes ischaemic heart disease with previous percutaneous coronary intervention ten years ago, dyslipidaemia, hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and knee osteoarthritis. His regular medications are aspirin, candesartan, rosuvastatin, metoprolol, metformin extended release, empagliflozin, and paracetamol extended release. He was advised in the pre-admissions clinic to cease empagliflozin for at least three days prior to the procedure.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits.
attributes.stemQuestion: Withholding empagliflozin for at least three days prior to colonoscopy reduces the risk of which one of the following complications?
attributes.explanation:
Euglycaemic diabetic ketoacidosis is a recognised complication of sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitor therapy, particularly in the context of physiological stress, fasting, or reduced carbohydrate intake, such as during bowel preparation for colonoscopy. Withholding these agents for at least three days prior to the procedure, as recommended by Australian guidelines, significantly reduces this risk. Euglycaemic diabetic ketoacidosis can present with normal or only mildly elevated blood glucose, making it more difficult to recognise and potentially life-threatening if not anticipated.

Hypoglycaemia is not a common complication of sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitors, especially when used in combination with metformin and in the absence of insulin or sulfonylureas. Withholding empagliflozin does not significantly impact hypoglycaemia risk in this context.

Urogenital candidiasis is a well-known adverse effect of sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitors, but the risk is related to ongoing use rather than short-term peri-procedural management. Withholding the medication for three days does not meaningfully reduce the risk of this complication during the peri-procedural period.

Acute kidney injury can occur with sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitors, particularly in the setting of hypovolaemia given their diuretic potential. The primary rationale for withholding these agents before colonoscopy is to prevent euglycaemic diabetic ketoacidosis, not acute kidney injury. Adequate hydration and monitoring renal function are also important.

Post-procedural bleeding is not associated with sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitor use.

Australian guidelines recommend withholding sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitors for at least three days prior to procedures involving fasting or bowel preparation to minimise the risk of euglycaemic diabetic ketoacidosis, which can be difficult to detect and manage.
learningPoints: Sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitors should be withheld for at least three days prior to procedures involving fasting or bowel preparation to reduce the risk of euglycaemic diabetic ketoacidosis.
Euglycaemic diabetic ketoacidosis can occur even with normal or mildly elevated blood glucose, especially during periods of physiological stress or reduced carbohydrate intake.
Withholding sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 inhibitors does not significantly reduce the risk of hypoglycaemia or urogenital infections in the peri-procedural period.
references: Medicines Advice Initiative Australia. Perioperative management of SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP-1 receptor agonists CATAG Practice Tool [Internet]. 2024 [cited 2025 Apr 21]. Available from: https://catag.org.au/wp-content/uploads/CATAG-Topic_Diabetes_Practice-Tool_F.pdf

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
